title: Distributed Systems — Staff+ FAANG Interview Prep
description: Comprehensive deep-dive into Distributed Systems at Staff+ FAANG engineering level. Covers foundations (CAP, FLP, fallacies), consensus (Paxos, Raft, Zab), time & coordination (clocks, leases, barriers), distributed storage (GFS, Colossus, HDFS, Ceph), distributed transactions (2PC, Percolator, Calvin, Saga), replication (quorum, CRDTs, multi-leader), partitioning (consistent hashing, rebalancing), consistency models, caching (Memcached, Redis, CDN), stream processing (Kafka, Flink, Beam), scheduling (Borg, K8s, Mesos), microservices & RPC (gRPC, Thrift, service mesh), security (mTLS, SPIFFE, OIDC), chaos engineering, global-scale systems, observability (tracing, logging, metrics), and anti-patterns — every topic a Staff+ engineer must own.
details:
Staff+ FAANG Interview Strategy: Distributed systems questions at Staff+ level test (1) Consistency vs availability tradeoffs — choose and defend with data, (2) Failure mode analysis — what breaks and how do you recover?, (3) Global-scale architecture — multi-region, edge, CDN, latency budgets, (4) Operational excellence — observability, capacity planning, incident response, (5) Protocol design — how services communicate, evolve, and degrade.

Company emphasis: Google: TrueTime, Spanner, Borg, global-scale infrastructure. Amazon: Dynamo (quorum, hinted handoff), multi-region (Global Tables), idempotency-first design. Meta: TAO graph cache, ZooKeeper for coordination, async replication at planetary scale. Netflix: Chaos engineering (Chaos Monkey), CDN (Open Connect), multi-region Cassandra.

Key principle: The best Staff+ distributed systems answers reveal experiential knowledge of failure modes — not just architectures that work when everything is fine, but what happens when nodes crash, networks partition, clocks skew, and bugs compound.

sections.title: 1. Distributed Systems Foundations: Fallacies, CAP, FLP & Beyond
sections.description:
FOUNDATIONS · LIMITS · CAP · FLP

Staff+ expectation: Distributed systems theory defines what’s possible. At Staff+ level, you don’t just recite CAP or FLP — you use them to make architectural decisions. “This system needs linearizability, so I accept the availability cost during partitions” or “I choose AP because my data model is convergent CRDTs.” You must understand the impossibility results and when they apply.

The Eight Fallacies (Sun, 1994): (1) The network is reliable, (2) Latency is zero, (3) Bandwidth is infinite, (4) The network is secure, (5) Topology doesn’t change, (6) There is one administrator, (7) Transport cost is zero, (8) The network is homogeneous. Staff+ insight: These cause more outages than software bugs. At Google, the #1 latency spike cause was assuming “the network is reliable” — a single optical transceiver failure degraded an entire cluster.

CAP Theorem (Brewer 2000): A store can provide at most two of: Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance. Staff+ insight: CAP is often misinterpreted. During a partition, choose C or A. When no partition, you can have both. The real question: what happens when the network fails? Mitigations: Quorum systems (W+R>N), CRDTs (merge without conflict), hinted handoff (Dynamo).

FLP Impossibility (1985): In async systems with one faulty process, consensus is impossible. Workaround: Every production implementation uses partial synchrony (timeouts + failure detectors).

Idempotency — The Distributed Superpower: Operations applied multiple times with same result. Implement via idempotency key + dedup window (24h). Amazon: every API carries Idempotency-Key header. Without it, retries cause duplicate charges.

1985
FLP impossibility proof
70%
Of FAANG incidents relate to partition handling
24h
Typical idempotency retention window

sections.title: 2. Consensus: Paxos, Raft, Zab & Implementations
sections.description:
CONSENSUS · PAXOS · RAFT · ZAB

Staff+ expectation: Consensus is the foundation of fault-tolerant systems. You must understand the replicated state machine pattern, leader election under partitions, and tuning knobs (heartbeat, election timeout, batch size).

Replicated State Machine: Clients submit commands to consensus module → ordered log → each replica applies same commands deterministically. Components: consensus module, persisted log, state machine, snapshot.

Raft: Nodes are followers/candidates/leaders. Election timeout (150-300ms randomized) → candidate → vote request → majority wins. Leader sends AppendEntries (heartbeat when idle). Entries committed when majority acknowledges. Pipeline: send next without waiting for previous ack. Batch: 8KB or 100ms window.

Multi-Paxos: Stable leader skips Prepare phase. Used by Google Chubby, DynamoDB.

Zab (ZooKeeper): Like Raft but handles leader transitions differently — leader ensures all follower logs match before accepting proposals.

etcdConsulZooKeeperChubbyDynamoDB
SystemAlgorithmUse caseThroughputLatency p99
RaftK8s metadata~50K/s~5ms
Raft+SWIMService mesh~30K/s~8ms
ZabHadoop/Kafka coord~10K/s~15ms
Multi-PaxosGFS lock~5K/s~20ms
Multi-PaxosGlobal KV>1M/s~10ms

Throughput limiters: Log fsync (NVMe ~10µs, EBS ~1ms), Network RTT (multi-region 50-200ms), Follower lag (majority must ack). Mitigations: read-only followers, zoned replication.

sections.title: 3. Time, Order & Coordination: Clocks, Leases & Barriers
sections.description:
TIME · CLOCKS · COORDINATION · LEASES

Staff+ expectation: Time is the hardest concept in distributed systems. You must understand physical clock failures (skew, NTP jumps, leap seconds), logical clocks (Lamport, vector), hybrid clocks (HLC, TrueTime), and coordination protocols (locks, leases, barriers).

Physical Time Failure Modes: Clock skew (NTP keeps within 1-50ms in DCs), NTP step correction (time can go backwards), leap second handling (Linux: jump in CLOCK_REALTIME). Rule: Use CLOCK_MONOTONIC for elapsed time, CLOCK_REALTIME only for human timestamps.

Lamport Clock: Each node maintains a counter. Increment on each event, include in messages. Recipient: max(own, received) + 1. Provides: if A happens-before B, clock(A) < clock(B). Does NOT provide: if clock(A) < clock(B) then A happens-before B (false positives possible).

Vector Clock: Array of N counters (one per node). Allows causality detection: if V(A) <= V(B) component-wise, A happens-before B. Used by Amazon Dynamo for conflict detection. Limitation: Vector size = node count. At 100K nodes, use dot clocks or version vectors.

Hybrid Logical Clocks (HLC): Physical time + logical counter. l = max(pt, l_received). HLC close to physical time while providing Lamport ordering. Used by CockroachDB.

TrueTime (Spanner): GPS + atomic clocks. Returns [earliest, latest] interval (uncertainty 1-7ms). Commit-wait: tt.after(s) ensures external consistency without distributed coordination.

Fencing Tokens: Client acquires lock → gets monotonic token → resource rejects stale tokens. Prevents problems from paused clients (GC, page fault) that continue after lock expiry.

sections.title: 4. Distributed Storage: GFS, Colossus, HDFS, Ceph & Object Stores
sections.description:
STORAGE · FILE SYSTEMS · OBJECT STORES

Staff+ expectation: Distributed storage is every FAANG’s infrastructure foundation. Understand the architecture of GFS (single master, 64MB chunks, append-heavy), Colossus (no single master, 1MB chunks, Reed-Solomon erasure coding), HDFS (NameNode bottleneck, small-files problem), Ceph (CRUSH eliminates central metadata), and S3-style object stores.

GFS (2003): 64MB chunks reduce metadata overhead. Single master is SPOF and bottleneck. 3x replication. Append-heavy optimized. Relaxed consistency (at-least-once).

Colossus (~2010): No single master (metadata in Bigtable). 1MB chunks for lower tail latency. Erasure coding (1.5x vs 3x = 50% savings). Client-driven replica placement. POSIX-like interface.

Ceph: RADOS core with CRUSH algorithm for placement (no central lookup). Provides object (RGW S3), block (RBD), and file (CephFS) from one cluster.

64MB
GFS chunk size (Colossus reduced to 1MB)
1.5x
Erasure coding overhead vs 3x replication
~10B+
Active files in Colossus clusters (est.)

sections.title: 5. Distributed Transactions: 2PC, Percolator, Calvin & Saga
sections.description:
TRANSACTIONS · 2PC · SAGA · PERCOLATOR

Staff+ expectation: Distributed transactions are the hardest problem. Understand 2PC (blocking on coordinator failure), Percolator (Google’s 2PC on Bigtable with TSO), Calvin (deterministic, no 2PC), and Saga (compensating transactions, the FAANG standard).

2PC: Phase 1: PREPARE all participants. Phase 2: COMMIT/ABORT. Problem: Coordinator failure blocks participants holding locks. 3PC adds pre-commit but re-introduces blocking under partitions.

Percolator (Google 2010): Global Timestamp Oracle + 2PC on Bigtable rows. Each row has data, lock, write columns. Client obtains start timestamp, writes to primary lock, then secondary locks. Commit: obtain commit timestamp, verify primary lock, write commit record.

Calvin (FaunaDB): All txns go through sequencer that orders globally. Participants execute deterministically. No 2PC. Limitations: Must declare R/W sets upfront. Bounded by sequencer speed and slowest participant.

AtomicityIsolationBlockingLatencyFAANG use
Feature2PCPercolatorCalvinSaga
All-or-nothingAll-or-nothingAll-or-nothingEventual
SerializableSISerializableNone
YesYes (locks timeout ~10s)NoNo
2 RTT + fsync2 RTT + TSO1 RTTPer-step
LegacyCockroachDB, TiDBFaunaDBMost microservices

sections.title: 6. Replication: Quorum, Chain, Multi-Leader, Async & CRDTs
sections.description:
REPLICATION · QUORUM · CRDT · CONSISTENCY

Staff+ expectation: Design the replication strategy for a global service. Understand single-leader (sync/async), multi-leader (conflict resolution), quorum (N=3, W=2, R=2), and CRDTs (merge without conflict).

Single-Leader: One node accepts writes. Synchronous = zero data loss + RTT latency. Asynchronous = lowest latency + data loss risk. Semi-sync = one sync follower, rest async.

Multi-Leader: All nodes accept writes. Conflict resolution via LWW (data loss), CRDT merge, or application-level. Attractive for multi-region but conflict resolution is very hard.

Quorum: N replicas, W write quorum, R read quorum. W+R > N = strong consistency. W=N,R=1 = fast reads. N=3,W=2,R=2 = typical Dynamo config. Sloppy quorum + hinted handoff solves the "lost quorum" problem during failures.

CRDTs: G-Counter (grow-only, element-wise max merge), PN-Counter (two G-Counters), OR-Set (add/remove with unique tags), LWW-Register (last-writer-wins). Used by: Redis Active-Active, Apple iCloud, Meta TAO.

W+R > N
Quorum condition for strong consistency
N=3,W=2,R=2
Default Dynamo quorum — balances latency/durability

sections.title: 7. Partitioning & Sharding: Consistent Hashing, Range & Rebalancing
sections.description:
PARTITIONING · SHARDING · REBALANCING

Staff+ expectation: Sharding is inevitable. Choose the right key (user ID vs timestamp), scheme (range vs hash vs consistent), and rebalancing strategy.

Sharding Key: Good = user ID (natural distribution, scoped queries). Bad = timestamp (hotspot on current shard), monotonic ID (all writes to last shard).

RangeHash mod NConsistent hashDirectory
SchemePlacementRange qryRebalancingExample
key range -> shardExcellentSplit shardBigtable, CockroachDB
hash(key)%NPoorK/N keys moveDynamoDB
Ring, nearest nodePoorK/N keysCassandra
Lookup: key->shardDependsUpdate lookupUber Schemaless

Consistent Hashing: Keys and nodes placed on ring [0, 2^32-1]. Key assigned to nearest clockwise node. Virtual nodes (100-200 per physical node) improve balance. Adding/removing a node moves only K/N keys.

Rebalancing: Fixed shard count (preshard, split overflowing) vs dynamic splitting (tablets split at 256-512MB, load balancer moves hot tablets). CockroachDB uses CPU+IOPS+disk load metric, rate-limits to 1 range/~5s.

sections.title: 8. Consistency Models: Linearizability, Sequential, Causal & Eventual
sections.description:
CONSISTENCY · LINEARIZABILITY · CAP

Staff+ expectation: Choose the right consistency model per operation — linearizable for payments, eventual for profiles. Understand the hierarchy and cost of each level.

Linearizability (External consistency): Once write completes, all subsequent reads see it. Requires consensus or TrueTime. Cost: highest latency. Spanner, etcd.

Sequential consistency: Ops appear in some sequential order (not necessarily real-time). Cheaper than linearizable but a write may not be visible to a subsequent read on a different node.

Causal consistency: Causally related ops seen in order by all nodes. Concurrent ops can appear in different orders. Vector clocks for detection.

Eventual consistency: Without new writes, replicas converge. No timing guarantee. Combined with session guarantees: read-your-writes, monotonic reads, monotonic writes, writes-follow-reads.

LinearizableSequentialCausalEventual
ModelReal-time?CostFAANG exampleSuitable for
YesHighestSpanner, etcdPayments, locks
NoHighCockroachDBInventory
NoMediumDynamoDB sessionSocial feeds
NoLowestDNS, CDN, DynamoDBProfiles, catalog

Staff+ insight: Most practical approach = consistency per operation. Payment ops linearizable. Product search eventual. User profiles read-your-writes. API contract specifies level per endpoint.
sections.codeBlock:
# Session-aware cache with read-your-writes
class SessionCache:
    def __init__(self, backend, local):
        self._backend = backend; self._local = local
    def get(self, sid, key):
        cached = self._local.get(sid, key)
        if cached: return cached
        val = self._backend.read(sid, key)
        self._local.put(sid, key, val)
        return val
    def put(self, sid, key, val):
        self._backend.write(sid, key, val)
        self._local.put(sid, key, val)  # immediate read-your-writes

# Consistency level per operation
# def process_payment(req):
#     # requires linearizability
#     return etcd.transaction(...)
#
# def get_catalog(req):
#     # eventual is fine
#     return cache.get(req.product_id)

sections.title: 9. Distributed Caching: Memcached, Redis Cluster, CDN & Invalidation
sections.description:
CACHING · REDIS · MEMCACHED · CDN

Staff+ expectation: Caching is the #1 performance optimization. Design the cache hierarchy (L1 local, L2 distributed), choose eviction (LRU, TinyLFU, ARC), and handle invalidation at internet scale.

Meta (Memcached): McDipper (auto-failover), McRouter (smart proxy + connection pooling), TAO (Memcached as L1 + MySQL as store, CDC-based invalidation via McSqueal).

Amazon (DAX & ElastiCache): DAX = write-through cache for DynamoDB (µs latency). ElastiCache = managed Redis/Memcached.

Cache Stampede: N concurrent requests miss cache, all hit origin. Mitigations: mutex per key, early refresh (refresh when TTL < 10%), probabilistic early recomputation (XFetch).

LRUTinyLFUARCLRU-K
PolicyMechanismScan-resistantBest for
Linked list+hashmapNoGeneral
Bloom-filter sketchYesHigh hit rate (Caffeine)
Adaptive LRU+LFUBestPostgreSQL, ZFS
Track K access timesYesWeb/CDN caches

Invalidation: TTL-based (simplest, eventual consistency), write-through (consistent + write latency), CDC-based (Debezium -> Kafka -> cache invalidation, ~10ms latency).

sections.title: 10. Stream Processing: Kafka, Flink, Kinesis & the Beam Model
sections.description:
STREAM PROCESSING · KAFKA · FLINK · BEAM

Staff+ expectation: Stream processing is the default at FAANG — batch is the exception. Understand Kafka’s architecture (log-segmented, zero-copy), Flink (stateful exactly-once), and the Beam model (event time, watermarks, triggers).

Kafka Architecture: Topics split into partitions (unit of parallelism + ordering). Log-segmented: each partition = directory of segment files. Zero-copy: sendfile() from page cache to socket. Replication: N replicas, ISR (in-sync replicas within 30s lag), min-ISR for durability vs availability tradeoff.

Flink: JobManager (scheduler, checkpoints) + TaskManagers (operators). State backend: in-memory, RocksDB, FsStateBackend. Chandy-Lamport snapshots for exactly-once. Kafka connector: offsets in Flink state → restore and rewind on failure.

Beam Model (Google Dataflow): Four dimensions: What (ParDo, GroupByKey), Where (event-time windowing: fixed, sliding, session), When (triggering: watermark, count, time), How (accumulation: discarding, accumulating). Watermark: confidence that all events with t < watermark have arrived.

~1.5T
Daily Kafka data volume at LinkedIn
69B
Daily Kafka messages at Uber
sections.codeBlock:
# Windowed aggregation (simplified Beam model)
from collections import defaultdict
import time

class WindowedAgg:
    def __init__(self, window_size=60):
        self._w = window_size; self._buckets = defaultdict(list)
        self._watermark = 0

    def add(self, event):
        ws = (event["ts"] // self._w) * self._w
        self._buckets[(ws, event["type"])].append(event["val"])

    def advance_watermark(self, wm):
        completed = []
        for (ws, typ), vals in list(self._buckets.items()):
            if ws + self._w < wm:
                completed.append({
                    "window_start": ws, "window_end": ws+self._w,
                    "type": typ, "count": len(vals),
                    "avg": sum(vals)/len(vals), "sum": sum(vals)
                })
                del self._buckets[(ws, typ)]
        self._watermark = wm
        return completed

# Kafka consumer group (confluent_kafka)
# from confluent_kafka import Consumer
# c = Consumer({"bootstrap.servers": "localhost:9092",
#               "group.id": "my-group",
#               "auto.offset.reset": "earliest"})
# c.subscribe(["my-topic"])
# while True:
#     msg = c.poll(1.0)
#     if msg:
#         print(f"{msg.topic()}[{msg.partition()}] @ {msg.offset()}: {msg.value()}")

sections.title: 11. Distributed Scheduling: Borg, Kubernetes & Mesos
sections.description:
SCHEDULING · ORCHESTRATION · BORG · K8S

Staff+ expectation: Schedulers manage 10K-50K machines. Understand Borg (preemption-driven utilization 80-90%), Kubernetes (predicates + priorities), and Mesos (resource offers for multi-framework).

Borg (Google): BorgMaster (Paxos leader), Borglet (node agent), Scheduler (feasibility + scoring). Key innovations: Priority + preemption (prod preempts batch for 80-90% utilization), resource estimation from historical data (20-50% better packing). Alloc = reserved resources (like K8s Namespace + Quota).

Kubernetes: API Server (etcd -> Raft), Scheduler (predicates: resource, affinity; priorities: least-requested, balanced), Kubelet (containerd/CRI-O), Controller Manager (Deployment, StatefulSet controllers). Max ~15K nodes per cluster recommended.

SchedulingPreemptionMax clusterLanguage
FeatureBorgKubernetesMesos
ScoringPredicates+PrioritiesResource offers
Yes (priority)Yes (1.18+)No
~50K machines~15K nodes~50K nodes
C++/GoGoC++

Mixed workloads: Static partitioning (wasteful), priority preemption (best utilization, batch can starve), resource quotas (min guaranteed + burstable), overcommitment (schedule extra batch assuming actual usage < requests, 20-50% overcommit at Google).

sections.title: 12. Microservices, Service Mesh & RPC: gRPC, Thrift, Protobuf
sections.description:
MICROSERVICES · SERVICE MESH · RPC

Staff+ expectation: Service boundaries are the hardest design decision. Understand gRPC (HTTP/2 multiplexing, protobuf encoding, streaming), Thrift (binary, framed transport), service mesh (envoy sidecar), and when NOT to use microservices.

Service Boundaries: Entity-based (UserService, OrderService) = chatty APIs. Domain-based (Bounded Context) = each service owns a complete subdomain. Amazon: one team per service, no shared DBs or code across teams.

gRPC: HTTP/2 transport (multiplexed streams, flow control). Protobuf encoding (3-10x smaller than JSON, 10-100x faster). Streaming: unary, server-streaming, client-streaming, bidirectional. When NOT to use: browser clients, simple CRUD, high throughput low payload.

SerializationSchemaSchema evolutionRPC stylesTransport
FeaturegRPCThriftJSON/REST
Protobuf (binary)BinaryJSON (text)
Required (.proto)Required (.thrift)Optional (OpenAPI)
Field numbers/reservedField IDsGraceful (extra fields ignored)
Unary/stream/duplexUnary/streamCRUD
HTTP/2TCPHTTP/1.1-3

Service Mesh (Istio): Envoy sidecar handles TLS, retries, circuit breaking, metrics, tracing. Pilot for service discovery + traffic management. Citadel for mTLS certs. Overhead: 5-15% latency/CPU.

sections.title: 13. Distributed System Security: mTLS, SPIFFE, OIDC & Secrets
sections.description:
SECURITY · mTLS · SPIFFE · SECRETS

Staff+ expectation: Design identity infrastructure for multi-service environments. Service-to-service auth (mTLS), workload identity (SPIFFE), user auth (OIDC), and secret distribution.

mTLS: Both client and server present TLS certs. Short-lived certs (24h). SPIFFE URI in SAN: spiffe://cluster.local/ns/default/sa/web. CPU overhead: 5-15% at high throughput. Use hardware acceleration or proxy termination.

SPIFFE/SPIRE: Standard for dynamic service identity. Node attestation (TPM, cloud metadata) + workload attestation (SA, cgroup, image hash). Issues SVIDs (X.509 or JWT).

OIDC: OAuth 2.0 + JWT ID token. Flow: client auths at IDP → ID token (JWT with sub, email, aud, exp) → backend validates signature, expiry, audience. JWT size 1-2KB. At 100K RPS, JWT validation adds 100MB/s parsing overhead. Use reference tokens for high-throughput internal services.

Secret Management: Never in code. Encrypted at rest. Audited access. Auto-rotated (DB passwords every 90 days). Just-in-time delivery. HashiCorp Vault: dynamic secrets, encryption-as-a-service, PKI for internal CA.

sections.title: 14. Chaos Engineering & Production Testing at Scale
sections.description:
CHAOS · FAILURE INJECTION · RESILIENCE

Staff+ expectation: Chaos engineering proactively tests system resilience. Design experiments, build failure injection infrastructure, and establish a resilience culture.

Principles (Principles of Chaos, 2018): (1) Define steady-state behavior (latency, error rate, throughput), (2) Vary real-world events (network partitions, DNS failures, resource exhaustion), (3) Run in production (staging doesn’t replicate real traffic), (4) Automate continuously, (5) Minimize blast radius (start with one instance).

Failure Injection Techniques: Network: packet loss (tc netem loss 0.1%), latency (tc netem delay 200ms), partition (iptables DROP). Resource: stress CPU, fill memory, fill disk, exhaust FDs. Process: kill -9, SIGSTOP (simulates GC pause), inject sleep via eBPF/aspect weaving. Dependencies: block DB port, fail DNS, expired TLS cert.

~50K
Chaos experiments/year at Netflix
3-5%
Latency increase from experiments

Maturity Model: L1 Ad-hoc (manual, game days yearly) → L2 Automated (staging, 10-20 scenarios) → L3 Continuous (production, canary, SLO verification) → L4 Self-healing (experiments trigger auto-mitigation).

Tools: Netflix Chaos Monkey (instance termination), Chaos Kong (region failure), Amazon FIS (managed), Gremlin (commercial).

sections.title: 15. Global-Scale Systems: CDNs, Multi-Region, Edge Computing & Geo-Distributed DBs
sections.description:
GLOBAL-SCALE · MULTI-REGION · EDGE · CDN

Staff+ expectation: Design global infrastructure serving users with sub-second latency everywhere. Understand three deployment approaches and CDN cache hierarchies.

Three Approaches: (1) Single-region + CDN: Static via CDN, dynamic goes to origin. Cross-continent latency 100-300ms. Regional outage = global outage. (2) Active-passive: Primary writes, standby idle. Failover on failure. Wastes capacity, async replication risks data loss. (3) Active-active: All regions serve traffic. Key challenge: data replication + conflict resolution. Single-master per partition (Spanner) vs multi-master with CRDTs (Dynamo Global Tables).

Global Traffic Management: DNS GSLB (returns different IPs by geo/latency/health. TTL-dependent failover: 30-300s) vs Anycast (same IP from multiple regions. BGP convergence: 30-120s. Good for DNS, CDN, DDoS scrubbing).

Edge Computing: Compute at CDN PoPs/ISP locations. Use cases: real-time video transcoding (Netflix Open Connect), IoT data filtering (AWS Wavelength), user personalization, bot/DDoS mitigation (Cloudflare Workers).

100-300ms
Cross-continent RTT
<50ms
CDN edge latency (static)
~5x
Active-active cost vs single-region

sections.title: 16. Staff+ Interview Strategy & System Design with DS Depth
sections.description:
INTERVIEW PATTERNS · STRATEGY · DEPTH

Staff+ expectation: DS questions embed consistency, consensus, and coordination into every Staff+ system design interview. The interviewer wants to see you reason about tradeoffs between consistency levels, replication strategies, and failure modes.

Common Staff+ Questions:

  • “Design a global payment system” — idempotency keys, Saga orchestration, multi-region replication, exactly-once processing.
  • “Design a real-time chat service” — vector clocks for ordering, CRDTs for message sync, WebSocket fanout, presence detection.
  • “Design a distributed lock service” — Raft/etcd, fencing tokens, lease expiry, clock skew handling.
  • “Design a monitoring system for 1M hosts” — hierarchical aggregation, streaming vs batch, dimensionality reduction, sampling.

Build vs Buy Framework: (1) Do you need strong consistency? Use Raft/etcd. (2) Can you tolerate eventual consistency? Use CRDTs or async replication. (3) Do you need distributed transactions? Prefer Sagas (compensating) over 2PC (blocking). (4) How many regions? 1-2 = active-passive. 3+ = active-active with single-master per partition. (5) What’s your latency budget? Cross-region consensus = 50-200ms. Local quorum = 5-15ms.

Key principle: The best answers reveal experiential knowledge of failure modes — not just architectures that work when everything is fine, but what happens when nodes crash, networks partition, clocks skew, and bugs compound. Always cite a specific observation from experience or well-known FAANG incidents.

Large-scale Cluster Management at Google with BorgGoogle Borg paper — resource management at scale
Release It! (Nygard)Circuit breakers, bulkheads, stability patterns
Building Microservices (Newman, 2nd ed)Service boundaries, communication patterns, testing
